question: Did the review adhere to pre-defined objectives and eligibility criteria?
answers:
The methods of this review were pre-specified according to a priori published protocol.

25. Pather P, Hines S, Kynoch K, Coyer F. The effectiveness of topical skin products in the treatment and prevention of incontinence-associated dermatitis: a systematic review protocol. JBI Database Syst Rev Implement Rep 2015;13(5):36-52.

question: Were the eligibility crieria appropriate for the review question?
answers:
The current review aimed to answer the following question: is the use of topical skin products in patients with incontinence effective in reducing the occurrence of IAD or the risk of developing IAD in adult patients with fecal and/or urinary incontinence and/or diarrhea and if so which products are the most effective?
The current review included studies conducted across all settings (acute care, intensive care, aged care, palliative care and community care) that included adult patients (both male and female) over the age of 18 years with urine incontinence, fecal incontinence with or without diarrhea, both urine and fecal incontinence with or without diarrhea, and diarrhea by any causal factor.

question: Were eligibility criteria unambiguous?
answers:
Types of participants
Adult patients over the age of 18 years with incontinence and/or diarrhea.
Types of intervention
Topical skin products as individual interventions or part of a skin care regimen.

question: Were all restrictions in eligibility crieria based on study characteristics appropriate (e.g. date, sample size, study quality, outcomes measured)? If yes, indicate which study characteristic was an inclusion/exclusion criteria
answers: Studies published from 1980 to 2016 were considered for inclusion in this review as this appears to be the period when initial literature on the subject of IAD was published

question: Were any restrictions in eligibility criteria based on sources of info appropriate (e.g. publication status or format, language, availability of data)?
answers:
studies in languages other than English were excluded as there were no resources available for translation. 

Limit to #(1980-present), English, adult participants >18 years of age, human research studies, limits#

question: Did the search include an appropriate range of databases/ electronic sources for published and unpublished reports?
answers: The databases searched included CINAHL, Cochrane Central Register of Controlled Trials, Embase, EBSCOHost, MEDLINE (via EBSCOHost), ProQuest Health and Medical Complete and Web of Science. The search for unpublished studies included Open Gray, Science.gov, Thesis and Dissertations, Clinical Trials.gov and International Standard Randomized Controlled Trial Number Registry.

question: Were methods additional to database searching used to identify relevant reports?
answers:
Third, the reference lists of all identified reports and articles were searched for additional studies.
13 studies selected from reference list searches of the included articles.

question: Were the terms and structure of the search strategy likely to retrieve as many eligible studies as possible?
answers:
Initial keywords used were: incontinence-associated dermatitis, perineal dermatitis, incontinence dermatitis, perineal rash, nappy/napkin rash and treatment.
The full search strategy is provided in Appendix I.

question: Were search strategy restrictions based on date, publication format, or language appropriate?
answers:
studies in languages other than English were excluded as there were no resources available for translation. 
Studies published from 1980 to 2016 were considered for inclusion in this review as this appears to be the period when initial literature on the subject of IAD was published

question: Were efforts made to minimise error in selection of studies?
answers: titles and abstracts were screened by two reviewers independently and articles for retrieval identified. Retrieved articles that met the inclusion criteria were then subject to the critical appraisal process.

question: Were efforts made to minimise error in data collection?
answers:
Quantitative data were extracted from papers
included in the review by two reviewers using the
standardized data extraction tool from JBI-MAStARI
(Appendix III). The data extracted included
specific details about the interventions, populations,
study methods and outcomes of significance to the
review question and specific objectives.

question: Were sufficient study characteristics considered for both review authors and readers to be able to interpret the results?
answers:
The data extracted included specific details about the interventions, populations, study methods and outcomes of significance to the review question and specific objectives.
Appendix V: List of study findings/conclusions/characteristics of included studies

question: Were all relevant study results collected for use in the synthesis?
answers:
Of the 10 studies included in this review, five focused on both cost effectiveness and clinical effectiveness with the others focusing solely on clinical benefits. A total of 804 participants across all studies were included in the final review.
Of these included studies, three were randomized controlled trials,15,28,31 five quasi-experimental studies,7,19,29,30,34 one prospective cohort descriptive study32 and one prospective descriptive two group study.33 One study was unpublished but presented as a poster.29 Of these, one study compared the effectiveness of zinc oxide oil (ZnO) against 3MTM Cavilon No Sting Barrier FilmTM (NSBF)

Appendix V: List of study findings/conclusions/characteristics of included studies

question: Was risk of bias (or methodological quality) formally assessed using appropriate criteria?
answers: Papers selected were assessed by two independent reviewers using the Joanna Briggs Institute Meta-Analysis of Statistics Assessment and Review Instrument (JBI-MAStARI)

question: Were efforts made to minimise error in risk of bias assessment?
answers: Papers selected were assessed by two independent reviewers using the Joanna Briggs Institute Meta-Analysis of Statistics Assessment and Review Instrument (JBI-MAStARI)

question: Did the synthesis include all studies that it should?
answers: Ten studies met the criteria for inclusion and were included in the review

question: Were all pre-defined analyses reported or departures explained?
answers: Meta-analysis of the extracted results was not possible due to the heterogeneity of the measurement tools, outcomes, time points and interventions

question: Was the synthesis appropriate given the nature and similarity in the research questions, study designs and outcomes across included studies?
answers:
Findings are narratively presented by types of interventions. Meta-analysis of the extracted results was not possible due to the heterogeneity of the measurement tools, outcomes, time points and interventions
It was not possible to pool studies to conduct meta-analysis or test for heterogeneity.

question: Was between-study variation (heterogeneity) minimal or addressed in the synthesis?
answers: It was not possible to pool studies to conduct meta-analysis or test for heterogeneity.

question: Were the findings robust, e.g. as demonstrated through funnel plot or sensitivity analyses?
answers: It was not possible to pool studies to conduct meta-analysis or test for heterogeneity.

question: Were biases in primary studies minimal or addressed in the synthesis?
answers: Methodological quality overall was poor

question: Did the interpretation of findings address all of the concerns identified in Domains 1 to 4?
answers:
Implications for research
The use of consistent terminology for IAD as
opposed to the wide variety of terms used to label
the condition will assist research and improve education
of healthcare providers. The testing, trialling
and wide use of an industry-approved IAD skin
assessment tool is also recommended to provide a
standardized tool that can be used across all settings
and by researchers for future studies. This will ensure
the correct diagnosis and treatment for IAD and
prevent it being misdiagnosed as a pressure injury.
It is also important to consider and include studies in
languages other than English, and an allocation of
funding for publishing in multiple languages and
translation should be included in research budgets
and proposals. Considering the absence of highquality
true RCTs and the methodology limitations
as evident in the studies included in this review, it is
clear that more research comparing effectiveness of
products with the measurement of common outcomes
that can be statistically measured is required.

question: Did the review authors provide a list of excluded studies and justify the exclusions?
answers:
Following critical appraisal, 10 studies were excluded (Appendix IV). Reasons for exclusion were outside inclusion date, not population of interest, methodology not research, laboratory studies with healthy volunteers using synthetic urine on forearms, study protocols with no results, not in English, not intervention or condition being reviewed and unclear measurement of IAD.

Appendix IV: Excluded studies
